main_summary.summary: Lisa Anderson Todd, interviewed June 24, 2013 at her home in Washington, D.C. by Emilye Crosby of the Southern Oral History Program for the Civil Rights History Project, recounts her path from a suburban New Jersey upbringing and Cornell undergraduate years into Mississippi Freedom Summer 1964. In summer 1963 she joined an Ecumenical Voluntary Service work camp at Tougaloo College and a follow-on American Friends Service Committee voter-registration project in Greensboro, North Carolina, meeting Fannie Lou Hamer in Ruleville and Bob Moses, Jane Stembridge, Memphis Norman, Rev. Ed King, and John Salter Jr. at Tougaloo. In 1964 she canvassed for the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party in Greenville under SNCC project director Charles E. Cobb Jr., working with Pam Trotman, Pat Vail, Muriel Tillinghast, and host family Jerry and Lamar Britton. She attended the MFDP challenge at the Democratic National Convention in Atlantic City and entered Stanford Law School that fall, later clerking for the National Lawyers Guild with Claudia Shropshire Morcom in summer 1965. Todd retired in 2006 to research and write a book on the MFDP convention challenge, drawing on her preserved diary, letters, the Anne Romaine oral histories, and the LBJ tapes.
key_themes: Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party convention challenge
Freedom Summer voter registration in Greenville
white volunteer experience and SNCC organizing
1963 Tougaloo work camp and Greensboro AFSC project
memory, archival research, and historical correction
main_summary.historical_significance:
Todd's testimony is distinctive for two reasons. First, she came to Freedom Summer with prior 1963 grounding in Mississippi (the Tougaloo Ecumenical Voluntary Service work camp) and Greensboro (an AFSC voter registration project that registered 803 people), which positioned her as an early SNCC applicant and gave her direct contact with Fannie Lou Hamer in Ruleville before Hamer's national prominence. She offers ground-level detail on the Washington County MFDP delegate-selection process and the friction between SNCC organizers and established Black Democratic Party operatives such as the local figure she identifies as Mr. Chappell.

Second, Todd is a participant-researcher: from 2006 onward she has spent years reconstructing the Atlantic City credentials-committee fight using the Anne Romaine oral histories, the LBJ telephone-tape archive, and the historiography around Joseph L. Rauh Jr., Walter Reuther, Hubert Humphrey, Bayard Rustin, Bob Moses, Courtland Cox, and Rep. Charles Diggs Jr. Her account explicitly distinguishes contemporaneous memory from later-acquired knowledge, names points where standard narratives (e.g., that LBJ's televised press conference was 'hastily called' to preempt Hamer's testimony) appear to rest on a single secondary source, and adds detail on the Reuther-Mondale Tuesday manipulation and the simultaneous two-room arrangement that produced the symbolic two-seat offer.

chapters.title: The Daughter of a Corporate Lawyer
chapters.summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby with John Bishop at her Washington, D.C. home in June 2013, Lisa Anderson Todd describes growing up in 1950s suburban Summit, New Jersey, the daughter of corporate lawyer Carl Anderson and a psychiatric social worker mother, with two older brothers and a Republican father who was wary of signing documents during the McCarthy scare.

chapters.title: The Iron Range and Hibbing High
chapters.summary: Todd traces her father's poor upbringing as one of six children in Hibbing, Minnesota, and her mother's benefit from the wealth of the Iron Ore Mines, which gave Hibbing a model high school with a swimming pool, before her mother moved from Hibbing junior college to the University of Minnesota.

chapters.title: I Don't Want a Vacation
chapters.summary: Todd recounts a summer with the Experiment in International Living in northern Sweden, following her older brother's American Friends Service Committee example, and deciding she wanted to do something rather than vacation, signing up for two 1963 work camps including an Ecumenical Voluntary Service project at Tougaloo College.

chapters.title: Dinner at Fannie Lou Hamer's Home
chapters.summary: At Bob Moses's invitation the group visited the SNCC office in Greenwood on August 1, 1963, and a few of them had dinner at Fannie Lou Hamer's home in Ruleville, where she told the story of the beating in Winona, then attended a mass meeting Todd found emotionally overwhelming.

chapters.title: 803 People Registered in Greensboro
chapters.summary: Todd describes moving on to a second American Friends Service Committee project in Greensboro, North Carolina, where eighteen volunteers lived in a church basement and did house-to-house canvassing in a receptive neighborhood with cooperative registrars, registering 803 people, mostly Democrats.

chapters.title: Special in Mississippi, Free in Greensboro
chapters.summary: Todd contrasts being a feared, conspicuous white person in Mississippi, seated on stage at a Jackson mass meeting addressed by Spottswood W. Robinson III, with the freedom of Greensboro, where late-night parties let her get to know black peers and concluded that relationships across the color line were no big deal between individuals though historically loaded.

chapters.title: Why Children Marched in Birmingham
chapters.summary: Returning to her 1963 Tougaloo experience, Todd recalls CORE doing the Jackson work, asking Dave Dennis why children were used in the Birmingham demonstrations and hearing they should learn to stand up for freedom early, and meeting figures like Memphis Norman and Rev. Ed King, whose scarred face she connected to his sacrifice as a white southerner.

chapters.title: An Engine on Fire to Jackson
chapters.summary: Todd describes reading of Medgar Evers's assassination in the New York Times, then flying to Jackson alone and seeing her plane's engine catch fire and turn back, a moment of truth before she continued, joining a group with black students Ivory Phillips and Thelma Sadberry and hearing evening speakers including Timothy Jenkins and Jane Schutt.

chapters.title: Outsiders Versus the Community
chapters.summary: Todd recounts the July to August 1963 lull in Jackson as the federal government pushed the movement from direct action toward less confrontational voter registration, the NAACP halting bail for arrested demonstrators and being criticized as outsiders, Charles Evers taking over, poor nonviolence-workshop turnout from students mistreated in jail, and the split between the local NAACP youth working with John Salter and Ed King and the national organization.

chapters.title: He Wouldn't Talk to Us
chapters.summary: Todd recalls Bob Moses on the Tougaloo campus declining to address the group because he had to leave, her annoyance when he turned up at a Student Center dance instead, and how his invitation to see the SNCC headquarters in Greenwood naturally followed, where the Delta work was being run.

chapters.title: A Short, Fat Woman with a Captivating Voice
chapters.topic: The Power of Fannie Lou Hamer
chapters.summary: Todd remembers having no idea who Fannie Lou Hamer was when Hamer cooked them dinner in Ruleville, then being struck by her captivating speech about her water bill rising and her home being shot into, her shade-tree parable, and the SNCC men like Charles McLaurin who came over from Greenwood.

chapters.part: Cornell and the Road to Freedom Summer
chapters.title: A Sorority That Pledged Jewish Girls
chapters.summary: Todd describes her difficult adjustment from an all-girls day school to Cornell, finding a diverse group when she pledged a sorority that rushed Jewish girls against national policy and cost Kappa Alpha Theta its charter after she left, and being an overachieving American Studies government major who took a race relations sociology course from Robin Williams that she thinks helped steer her toward the two summer work projects.

chapters.part: Cornell and the Road to Freedom Summer
chapters.title: Choosing Tougaloo Over Brownsville
chapters.summary: Todd explains choosing Tougaloo over a Texas project because the civil rights situation was in the news, framing 1963 as learning about the movement rather than activism, her supportive parents who let her go on her own savings, and her father's United Negro College Fund and Lutheran Church involvements.

chapters.part: Cornell and the Road to Freedom Summer
chapters.title: Back at Cornell a Different Person
chapters.summary: Todd describes returning to Cornell a different person, becoming active with Cornell United Religious Work under Paul Gibbons, putting out a newsletter and working for passage of the Civil Rights Act, and recalls meeting Jane Stembridge at Tougaloo working on literacy books, who ensured the group received SNCC literature to start friends-of-SNCC chapters Todd never formed, and who remembered Bob Moses as a saint in the summer of 1960.

chapters.part: Cornell and the Road to Freedom Summer
chapters.title: A Letter from Walter Tillow
chapters.summary: Todd describes lobbying on the Civil Rights Act during a spring Washington trip, then applying to work for SNCC in fall 1963 and receiving a letter from Walter Tillow describing a planned project of Freedom Schools, legal resources, and voter registration, with a strange note that they might find a way to pay her.

chapters.part: Cornell and the Road to Freedom Summer
chapters.title: Where's Mother? She's Not Feeling Well
chapters.summary: Todd recounts weighing becoming a civil rights worker against law school after taking the LSATs, debating during the summer whether to stay in Mississippi, and coming home by bus to Summit where her father said her mother was upset, prompting her to choose Stanford, after three students recruiting MFDP support told a reporter she planned to stay and her mother read it in the paper, a strain eased by a parents group and the Sheffield family whose daughter Nancy volunteered in Greenville.

chapters.title: To Be There Would Be Important
chapters.summary: Todd describes arriving late to the Oxford orientation after a friend's wedding, riding with Cornell students bound for the Fayette County Tent City project, hearing Bob Moses in his overalls tell volunteers not to expect to accomplish much but to write congressmen and newspapers, and being lectured on nonviolence by Vincent Harding and Bruce Hanson before the role-playing on the lawn where Newsweek's Carl Fleming punched her in the stomach as she played victim.

chapters.title: The Day I Arrived in Mississippi
chapters.summary: Todd describes the disappearance of Chaney, Schwerner, and Goodman the Saturday she arrived, her fear that they would all be victimized, and ending up assigned to voter registration in Greenville, a relatively moderate city, partly because her Greensboro experience suited her for it and the fields were too dangerous for women elsewhere.
related_events: murder of Chaney Schwerner and Goodman

chapters.title: I Felt That I Had Status
chapters.summary: Todd reflects on feeling privileged in voter registration for the access it gave her to black adults and politics, compared with the freedom school teachers, all white women, working with children, and names canvassing partners including Pam Trotman, a Howard graduate recruited by Stokely Carmichael, and older men Charles Moore and James Williams.

chapters.title: Who Was the Project Director?
chapters.summary: Todd describes confusion over the Greenville project director, recalling George Rawick of the University of Chicago for the first month and thinking Charlie Cobb directed it, only to learn through research and from Dottie that Muriel Tillinghast was project director, with Cobb directing Washington, Sharkey, and Issaquena counties while traveling the state.

chapters.title: I Felt Safe in the Black Community
chapters.summary: Todd describes feeling safe and less at risk in Greenville, moving freely in the black community and never going to the white side, canvassing all day in the heat, and the significance of living with a black family after first sleeping on the Freedom House floor, sharing quarters with Valerie and Lynn who worked in research.

chapters.title: Don't Use Too Much Water
chapters.summary: Todd recounts a widow who asked them to leave for fear of hosting three white women, moving to the independent Mrs. Harden whose neighbor said Uncle Toms wanted to run her out of church, then to Jerry and Lamar Britton's air-conditioned brick house, an open home where she shared a room with Valerie Horton and Lynn Hamilton.

chapters.title: A Clipboard and a Freedom Registration Form
chapters.summary: Todd describes no fixed routine, dividing the city at the office, the transportation problem that meant walking or catching rides with black taxi drivers, and how her canvassing shifted from neutral responses about courthouse registration to signing people up for the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party, which required only a signature, not literacy.

chapters.title: This Form Is Not Going Anywhere
chapters.summary: Todd contrasts the deterrent of official registration, with its literacy tests, constitutional interpretation, and two weeks of name publication, against the privacy of freedom registration, explaining people signed when assured the form would stay private, that Pat handled the returned forms, and that black-and-white canvassing teams avoided deference to a white authority figure.

chapters.title: In Charge of a Precinct
chapters.summary: Todd recalls the rumor of a white volunteer who left pregnant with a black volunteer, the six Greenville precincts where she ran one with a workshop at Mr. Williams's house drawing twenty people, and the county convention where local Democrat Mr. Chappell failed to install his chairman but moved that all present precinct delegates become county delegates, mixing traditional leaders with strong MFDP people while women resented that establishment figures stepped in only at the end.

chapters.title: Women More Able to Stand Up
chapters.summary: Todd names strong Greenville leaders working with SNCC and the FDP including Dorothy Jones, who worked for the Hodding Carter family, Edna Morton, and Willie Rollins, and reflects, as a matter historians consider in retrospect, that women were more involved because men risked being lynched while women were more able to act for civil rights.

chapters.title: Fannie Lou Hamer Led the Singing
chapters.summary: Todd describes the Delta's second congressional district caucus held at Greenville's Friendship Baptist Church, a former movie theater, where Fannie Lou Hamer led the singing and gave Todd a big hug recalling her 1963 home visit, and explains the MFDP was built to parallel the regular Mississippi Democratic Party under matching state-law procedures.

chapters.title: A Recommended List, Not Yet Delegates
chapters.summary: Todd recalls her enthusiasm seeing people participate in politics, explains the district caucus produced a recommended delegate list for the state convention that would elect the 68 national delegates, and that she was pleased Mr. Chappell was not recommended though he got nominated by another district and still failed to be elected to Atlantic City.

chapters.title: Not Hippie Scruffy Kids
chapters.topic: Mrs. Britton and the Percy Library Oral Histories
chapters.summary: Todd relays Mrs. Britton's recorded view that the volunteers were nice rather than scruffy, her worry for them coming home at night, her husband's death that fall when the project sent notes, and the roughly 1978 oral histories in Greenville's Percy Memorial Library, done she thinks by Daisy Bates, that mostly interviewed schoolteachers.

chapters.part: The Pool, the Militants, and Old Friends
chapters.title: Finding New Voices
chapters.summary: Todd clarifies she was not at the state convention though she long thought she was, then relays Charlie Cobb's instruction to work around established leaders to find new voices, which she later understood as empowerment, and Martin Luther King's idea of the beloved community as the goal of nonviolence and reconciliation, words they never used at the time.

chapters.part: The Pool, the Militants, and Old Friends
chapters.title: This Nonviolence Stuff Is Not Working
chapters.summary: Todd recalls militant young men on the Greenville project, apparently SNCC staff assigned by COFO Jackson, who spoke of anger and self-defense and disparaged Bob Moses as on his way out, and a first-week demonstration at the federal building, instigated by the local Mississippi Student Union, where she prepared to be arrested but nothing happened.

chapters.part: The Pool, the Militants, and Old Friends
chapters.title: That Couldn't Be True
chapters.summary: Todd describes Bob Moses in Oxford disbelieving that Greenville had demonstrated despite instructions not to, the two militant men's push to picket the black swimming pool and later again at the federal building for protection, the dilemma of supporting local people who wanted their support, and one man phoning the police chief that they would arrive in fifteen minutes.

chapters.part: The Pool, the Militants, and Old Friends
chapters.title: What Did We Do for Fun?
chapters.topic: Cafe Talk and a Doctor's Picnic
chapters.summary: Todd answers what they did for fun, sharing beer and movement talk in cafes apart from Charlie Cobb, who never joined them, a July 4th barbecue picnic at Dr. Frisbee's home for thirty-five volunteers among Greenville's established black middle class, and the time Dorothy Jones invited them to swim in Hodding Carter's pool while the family was away.

chapters.part: The Pool, the Militants, and Old Friends
chapters.title: Men on Horseback and a Revolver in the Drawer
chapters.summary: Todd describes the whole project swimming in Hodding Carter's pool in their clothes until two or three men rode in on horseback to protect the Carters, the party disbanding, and Barbara and Nancy staying overnight to find the house an arsenal with a revolver in the nightstand, an event everyone including Charlie Cobb remembered.

chapters.part: The Pool, the Militants, and Old Friends
chapters.title: A Black Armband at Coleman High
chapters.summary: Todd tells how she pieced together that Johnny Frazier, who addressed her 1963 group as head of the Greenville NAACP Youth Chapter, had worn a black armband to Coleman High School over the Brown decision and was suspended, with Hodding Carter writing an editorial, and was readmitted only when his aunt threatened to sue to integrate the white school.

chapters.part: The Pool, the Militants, and Old Friends
chapters.title: Thank You for What You Did
chapters.summary: Todd recounts reuniting with Johnny Frazier at a reunion sponsored by his daughter Nishani Frazier, his bear hug thanking her, and meeting Dorie Ladner around 1994 or 1995 at a Biograph Theater showing of Freedom on My Mind where Heather Booth greeted Senator Paul Simon, with Ladner thanking her for being a 1964 summer volunteer.

chapters.title: Freelance on the Boardwalk
chapters.summary: Todd describes deciding to attend the convention because Atlantic City was near Summit, driving down with Pat and missing Fannie Lou Hamer's Saturday testimony, sleeping at the Union Baptist Temple church where the MFDP met, and working freelance walking the boardwalk to urge delegates to vote for the MFDP without seeking an official role.

chapters.title: Ella Baker Ran the Lobbying
chapters.summary: Todd explains she later learned Ella Baker organized the state-delegation lobbying from the MFDP Washington office, with help from the woman who wrote Rauh's brief and Charlene Cranston Young arranging transportation at the Gem Hotel, while she and Pat never went there and instead attended open meetings at the church through the Tuesday credentials decision.

chapters.title: There Was No Compromise
chapters.summary: Todd recounts standing in an aisle hearing prominent people urge acceptance of what they called the compromise, arguing there was no compromise because there were not two sides reaching agreement, that she knew the MFDP should be seated, that few delegates spoke in the open discussion, and that the no vote came in a closed room of only the 68 delegates.

chapters.title: King Was Hushed Out
chapters.topic: The Church Meetings and a Confidential Caucus
chapters.summary: Todd describes the sequence after the credentials decision: Joseph Rauh recommending the delegates support the offer, Martin Luther King not allowed to speak on Tuesday afternoon, Edith Green and other supporters meeting separately and agreeing to keep discussions confidential, and ultimately filing no minority report because Rauh decided he lacked the numbers.

chapters.title: To Find Out Why We Lost
chapters.topic: Beginning the Book and the Research
chapters.summary: Todd explains retiring on April 1, 2006 to write something more interesting, advised by writer friends to produce a fast memoir from her transcribed letters and diary, her real aim being to understand the MFDP convention challenge and why they lost after leaving disillusioned, and how her research moved from Taylor Branch to Anne Romaine's oral interviews at UNC and the LBJ Library, where the Johnson tapes were eye-opening, into a 250-page paper criticized as repetitive that John Dittmer advised needed footnotes.

chapters.title: Johnson Did Not Want a Race Election
chapters.summary: Todd lays out her research findings on why Johnson opposed seating the Freedom Democrats: beyond the traditional fear of losing southern electoral votes to Barry Goldwater, the Rochester and Harlem riots had made backlash part of the lexicon, and Johnson, advised by Governor John Connally and worried about his Texas votes after the Civil Rights Act, did not want the election to be about race.

chapters.title: He Was Not One Hundred Percent for the Freedom Democrats
chapters.summary: Todd assesses Joseph Rauh as critically important, having structured the credentials hearing around Fannie Lou Hamer's testimony and won the large ballroom, but serving two roles, the MFDP and his own standing in the Democratic Party, and astoundingly offering to withdraw yet advising against it lest Kunstler and Kinoy take over and he lose control.

chapters.title: They Did Not Want a Floor Fight
chapters.topic: A Vehicle to Reform the Party
chapters.summary: Todd explains the National Democratic Party's overriding fear of a floor vote that would expose its northern pro-civil-rights and southern segregationist wings, Rauh's wish to reform the party using the Freedom Democratic Party as a vehicle to force change, and Walter Reuther's early lack of enthusiasm as a Johnson ally who saw the political problem.

chapters.title: The Names Kept Changing
chapters.summary: Todd recounts Courtland Cox's openness about Congressman Charles Diggs requesting the list of credentials committee members supporting the MFDP, Bob Moses's reluctance, the roughly fifteen names handed over, overnight calls threatening supporters, and Johnson aides who pegged every member exactly, with documentation of LBJ calling the governor of Iowa and a Colorado reporter and pressure on a New York delegate who was Mayor Wagner's secretary and a black woman from Maryland, all of which she set up her book to tell day by day to give the Freedom Democrats their due credit.

chapters.title: It All Happened Because of Reuther
chapters.summary: Todd argues Hubert Humphrey acted only on Monday, pressuring state delegations to drop a roll-call vote to please Johnson and secure his vice presidency, but it was Walter Reuther, arriving Tuesday in the night and working the Mondale subcommittee, whose manipulation produced the symbolic at-large two-seat offer by holding two meetings at once so the credentials committee voted while the leadership was tied up, refusing to negotiate, with the contested claim that Aaron Henry and Ed King accepted the offer and Bayard Rustin conducting the leadership meeting attended by King and Andrew Young.

chapters.title: He Was Ready to Cut It Off
chapters.summary: Todd presents her finding that Johnson's press conference was scheduled the day before Fannie Lou Hamer's testimony, contradicting the gospel from Rauh via Anne Romaine that he called it hurriedly, so he was ready to cut off the televised testimony, and reflects that she needs to settle her main points before talking publicly about the book.

chapters.part: A Lawyer's Life and Later Reflections
chapters.title: Six Hundred People at the Church
chapters.summary: Todd describes wanting to set the historical record straight, then a fundraiser at the Central Presbyterian Church in Summit where she and a neighbor who volunteered in Drew addressed 600 people to fund the Herbert Lee Memorial Freedom Center, which she helped organize by enlisting the established Greenville leaders she had called Uncle Toms, the center taking shape with Delta Ministry and volunteer lawyers, young doctor Matthew Page as president and Charles Moore, later a longtime city councilman, as vice president.

chapters.part: A Lawyer's Life and Later Reflections
chapters.title: Across the Country to Stanford
chapters.summary: Todd describes driving to Stanford law school, living in graduate housing with Karen Davis whose younger sister Linda taught in a Ruleville Freedom School, joining the Law Students Civil Rights Research Council, and applying to return to Mississippi as a law clerk before being reassigned from the Lawyers' Committee for sounding militant and failing to defer to lawyers she found naive, admitting she disassociated from the movement during law school out of fear of flunking out as her brother had at Minnesota, reading only the San Francisco Chronicle.
keywords: Law Students Civil Rights Research Council

chapters.part: A Lawyer's Life and Later Reflections
chapters.title: Lisa, Get the Gun
chapters.summary: Todd describes her 1965 reassignment to the National Lawyers Guild, living in Tougaloo with Claudia Shropshire Morcom and clerking for volunteer lawyers evaluating Title III public facilities county by county in places like Madison County, Greenwood, Greenville, and Jackson, and Morcom's later story of yelling for a gun they did not have at a feared peeping Tom.

chapters.part: A Lawyer's Life and Later Reflections
chapters.title: Red Baiting to Be Dismissed
chapters.topic: Returning to Greenville as a Lawyer
chapters.summary: Todd recalls Bob Moses's orientation lecture that red-baiting was past and the movement would accept help from anyone, her own dismissal of being called communist during the summer, and how far she had disassociated, returning to Greenville for legal work without looking anyone up or asking about the Herbert Lee Center she had helped found.

chapters.part: A Lawyer's Life and Later Reflections
chapters.title: The Atmosphere of the Agency Changed
chapters.summary: Todd describes clerking at the Office of Economic Opportunity, choosing Washington over New York, and her first job at the United Planning Organization, a Community Action Agency writing delegate-agency contracts, where within two years she felt the effects of black power and grew less welcome as a white worker under executive director Wiley Branton, whom she admired, and resigned.

chapters.part: A Lawyer's Life and Later Reflections
chapters.title: A Board of Contract Appeals Judge
chapters.topic: Government Contracts and the Rule of Law
chapters.summary: Todd traces entering government contracts law with Trovon Bower and Morgan, Lewis and Bockius, then qualifying under the Contract Disputes Act of 1978 to become an administrative judge on the NASA Board of Contract Appeals with worldwide jurisdiction, hearing contractor disputes in full trial-type proceedings, and reflects that after years discussing nonviolence and civil disobedience her judicial obligation was to apply the rule of law, offering a fair hearing even when a victimized contractor's situation cried out and in the end she had to say no.

chapters.part: A Lawyer's Life and Later Reflections
chapters.title: A Whole New World
chapters.summary: Todd describes getting the book idea from a friend who saw her large civil rights library, how revisiting the Mississippi summer project through the Veterans of the Mississippi Civil Rights Movement conferences in Jackson opened a whole new world, being meticulously mentored over tea by Mary King, and her view of the legacy: that Freedom Summer did not affect the 1964 Civil Rights Act, done before they arrived, but contributed to the Voting Rights Act and party reform she traces toward the 2008 election of Obama.
keywords: Veterans of the Mississippi Civil Rights Movement

chapters.part: A Lawyer's Life and Later Reflections
chapters.title: We Made a Difference
chapters.topic: Legacy and a Message for the Young
chapters.summary: Todd insists the work was done by many, the SNCC activists in real danger and the roughly 500 college-age volunteers rather than the often-cited thousand, that things are better and young people should not be cynical about a dysfunctional Congress, and offers what she would tell students: that change comes when a single person finds a passion and reaches out to build a larger group, what might be called community organizing, recalling one high school history class that lit up when she read from her diary.

chapters.part: A Lawyer's Life and Later Reflections
chapters.title: I Didn't Think You Asked If I Was Scared
chapters.topic: Fear, Safety, and the VFW Knife
chapters.summary: Todd answers that she was scared at the beginning but not when she left Oxford, unsettled by cars circling the Freedom House that turned out to be police, and reassured by her Greenville assignment where no Klan or citizens council was allowed to organize, naming her standard worry as pickup trucks with rifle racks at night and her closest brush with violence at a VFW fundraiser dance sponsored by Charles Moore, the only white woman there, when one man pulled a knife after she danced with another, before the interview closes with thanks.

loc_video.caption: Lisa Anderson Todd oral history interview conducted by Emilye Crosby in Washington, D.C., 2013 June 24
